1: The oracle of Egypt. Massa (oracle/burden) opens this unit as it opened ch 13. YHWH is flagged -- the divine personal name is the one coming on a swift cloud to Egypt. The cloud-rider imagery evokes Ps 68:4 and 104:3, where the LORD rides the clouds; that title belongs to YHWH alone. Idols of Egypt (elilim) -- worthless things, cognate with nothing -- tremble before the LORD coming in theophanic judgment.
2: I will stir up Egyptians against Egyptians. Divine sovereignty over internal Egyptian strife -- the LORD is the active agent behind civil conflict that appears merely political. The Hebrew yasseti (I will stir up) makes YHWH the prime mover. The structure moves from the high-level announcement (v1) to the specific mechanisms of judgment (vv2-4).
3: Egypt's spirit will drain away within it. Ruach is flagged (disp 4) -- rendered spirit. Here, ruach refers to Egypt's national capacity for wisdom, courage, and governance; it is being emptied out (vavuqqah: drained). The MKT's drain away captures the emptying image. Egypt turns to enchanters, mediums, and spiritists -- replacing the LORD's counsel with occult substitutes. Idols (v1) and spirit (v3) both fail simultaneously.
4: I will hand Egypt over to a harsh master; a cruel king will rule over them. YHWH is flagged as the one making this pronouncement. Melek (king, disp 2) -- the cruel king is deliberately unnamed, keeping the oracle at the level of type (Assyrian kings -- Esarhaddon, Ashurbanipal -- are historical referents). The MKT preserves the Hebrew pair adon qasheh (harsh master) and melek az (cruel king).
5: The Nile's drying is the centerpiece of Egypt's agricultural judgment -- Egypt is the gift of the Nile, and without it there is no harvest. The MKT's parched and drained captures the two stages of water loss. The LORD does not need armies when he can remove the water: judgment works through the withdrawal of created goods.
6: The channels and branches of the Nile system are specified. Reeds and rushes -- the papyrus-producing plants, Egypt's primary writing material and export commodity -- wither and rot. Judgment works from the main channel outward, drying up the entire delta system.
7: The papyrus reeds are the most economically significant detail: Egypt's papyrus trade supplied the ancient world's writing materials. The MKT's crops planted along its banks broadens the picture from papyrus to the full agricultural system. When the Nile withdraws, everything planted along it dies in sequence from the main channel outward.
8: The fishermen mourn -- hook, net, and seine fishermen whose livelihood depends on the Nile's abundance. The mourning (avelu) is the formal vocabulary of communal lamentation; this is more than economic loss. The MKT distinguishes lament (for hook fishermen) and languish (for net fishermen), following the Hebrew verbal distinctions.
9: Linen workers and weavers -- Egypt's other major export industry after papyrus. Egyptian fine linen was famous throughout the ancient world. Both the raw material workers (combed flax) and the finished-goods manufacturers (weavers) are put to shame -- the economic ripple from water loss reaches every major industry.
10: Egypt's foundations will be crushed; all who work for hire will be sick at heart. Nefesh is flagged (disp 3) -- the MKT renders the workers as sick at heart. The Hebrew kol-sachire nefesh is literally all the hired-ones of soul -- those who hire out their whole vital selves for wages. Sick at heart captures not just economic distress but deep existential anguish when life-defining work is destroyed.
11: The wisest of Pharaoh's counselors give worthless advice. The confrontation between Egypt's legendary wisdom and the word of the LORD. Egyptian wisdom was renowned (1 Kings 4:30); Isaiah ironically dismantles it. The princes of Zoan (Tanis, capital of the Nile delta) and their wise men cannot decode what the LORD is doing -- wisdom that lacks the fear of the LORD is hollow at the critical moment.
12: YHWH is flagged -- the divine name is the one whose plan the Egyptian wise men cannot decode. The rhetorical challenge -- where are they now? -- is devastating: the wisdom tradition collapses when confronted with genuine divine action. Yada (know, disp 2) appears in make known (causative): the wise men cannot cause-to-be-known what the LORD has decreed.
13: The leaders of Egypt have led her astray. Those who should be the anchor of Egyptian governance have become stumbling-blocks. The princes of Zoan are treated as fools (nivalu) and Memphis's princes are deluded (hitvu). The geographic diversity of failed leadership (Zoan in the delta, Memphis in the center) suggests all of Egypt's governing centers have collapsed simultaneously.
14: YHWH is flagged; ruach is flagged (disp 4) -- a second occurrence, now a spirit the LORD actively pours in (massakh: mixed/poured). This is judicial deception -- when a people persistently refuse truth, the LORD gives them over to confusion (cf. 1 Kings 22:22; Rom 1:24-28). The MKT's spirit of confusion distinguishes this active divine gift of disorientation from the natural political chaos of v2.
15: There will be nothing Egypt can accomplish -- neither head nor tail, neither palm branch nor reed. The head-tail and palm branch-reed idioms (already used in 9:14-15 for Judah) reappear for Egypt -- the judgment vocabulary is transferable across nations. From top to bottom, from the honorable to the worthless, nothing remains capable of action.
16: YHWH is flagged -- the hand of the LORD is the source of Egypt's terror. The comparison to frightened women is a stock ancient Near Eastern image of military collapse -- the reversal of martial confidence into panic. The phrase in that day (repeated five times in vv16-23) marks this section as eschatological: a future period when the LORD's redemptive purposes for Egypt unfold.
17: YHWH is flagged -- the land of Judah becomes a source of dread to Egypt because of the LORD's purposes (etzah: counsel/plan). The mention of Judah is startling: tiny Judah, perennially Egypt's dependency, becomes frightening to Egypt because it carries the LORD's counsel. Whoever stands with the LORD of hosts becomes formidable to all who oppose him.
18: YHWH is flagged. In that day five cities in Egypt will speak the language of Canaan -- Hebrew, the language of the covenant people -- and swear loyalty to the LORD of hosts. Five Egyptian cities speaking Hebrew and swearing allegiance to YHWH is the oracle's most extraordinary future-hope element. One city is called the City of Destruction (Hebrew heres) or the City of the Sun (Heliopolis) in a manuscript variant -- the textual ambiguity may be deliberate wordplay. The MKT follows the heres reading.
19: YHWH is flagged twice -- an altar dedicated to the LORD at Egypt's center, and a standing stone at Egypt's border dedicated to the LORD. Both altar and matsevah (standing stone) are covenant-marker forms. In the Torah, standing stones mark patriarchal covenant events (Gen 28:18); here Egypt erects its own. The MKT's in the heart of Egypt emphasizes that this is not a peripheral border cult but a central worship practice.
20: YHWH is flagged twice -- the altar and pillar serve as sign and witness to the LORD of hosts in the land of Egypt. The covenant-legal language is applied to Egypt's worship. When Egypt cries out to the LORD, he will send a savior (moshia) and champion -- the same word used for the judges who delivered Israel (Judg 3:9,15). The MKT's deliver and rescue them preserves the doubled Hebrew verb of salvation.
21: YHWH is flagged three times. The LORD will make himself known to Egypt, and the Egyptians will acknowledge the LORD in that day. Yada (know, disp 2) appears twice -- the LORD making himself known and the Egyptians knowing/acknowledging him. The second yada is covenantal acknowledgment-knowing, not mere intellectual awareness. The Egyptians will worship with sacrifice, grain offering, vows fulfilled -- the full vocabulary of covenant worship applied to the former oppressors of Israel.
22: YHWH is flagged twice. The LORD will strike Egypt -- striking and healing. The paradox of divine chastisement as ultimately remedial. The repeated striking-and-healing is not cruelty but covenantal discipline that produces repentance. The Egyptians will shuv (return/repent) to the LORD -- the core word for covenant restoration -- and the LORD will heal them with active therapeutic care.
23: In that day there will be a highway from Egypt to Assyria. The highway connecting the scattered (11:16; 40:3) now connects Egypt and Assyria -- Israel's two great enemies -- in shared worship. The three nations together avad (serve/worship) the LORD. The MKT's will worship together captures the shared cultic life the highway enables. The nations who enslaved and threatened Israel are now co-worshippers.
24: In that day Israel will be the third alongside Egypt and Assyria, a blessing in the midst of the earth. The three-in-a-row structure -- Egypt, Assyria, Israel -- is itself remarkable: Israel in the middle, no longer defined by opposition to these nations but by shared membership in the LORD's community of blessing. The blessing in the midst of the earth echoes the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3) now extended to include Israel's former oppressors as co-recipients.
25: YHWH is flagged -- the divine name as the blesser. Egypt my people -- the covenant term ami (my people), used exclusively in the OT for Israel, is here applied to Egypt. Assyria the work of my hands -- creator-language. Israel my inheritance -- land-gift language. Three equally honorific divine titles for three nations. This verse is the zenith of Gentile-inclusion prophecy in the first half of Isaiah (chs 1-39), surpassing even 2:2-4.
